cards.text:
Real Image Virtual Image
A real image is formed, when two or more reflected rays intersects each other at a point. A virtual image is formed when two or more reflected rays appear to intersect or meet at a point.

Laws Of Reflection :The two laws of reflection of light are:-

  1. The incident ray, the normal and the reflected ray lie in the same plane.
  2. The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection of a ray of light striking a smooth polished surface i.e., {tex}\angle{/tex}i = {tex}\angle{/tex}r.
Reflection: It is a phenomenon of light in which a ray of light from one medium strikes a smooth polished surface and returns back to the same medium.
Divergent beam: When the rays actually diverge or appear to diverge from a point, then the beam containing such rays are called divergent beam and rays are called divergent rays.
Convergent beam: When the rays actually meet or appear to meet at a point, then the beam containing such rays are called convergent beam and rays are called convergent rays.
Parallel beam: When the rays which constitute the beam are parallel to one-another, then they are called as parallel beam of light.
Source Of Light: Any object which has an ability of producing light energy at its own is called as the source of light. e.g., Sun, Firefly, Battery, Cell Candle, Bulb etc.
Light: Light is a form of energy, which induces the sensation of vision in our eyes and makes us able to see various things present in our surroundings.

Kinds Of Sources Of Light:  

  1. Natural Sources:- These include all those sources which are natural and possess their own light for example stars, firefly etc.
  2. Artificial sources:- These include all those sources which are man-made or artificial but possess the ability of emitting light for example candle, lantern, moon etc.

Properties of Light :

  • Light waves are electromagnetic waves, so they do not need any material medium for their propagation.
  • Light can travel in vacuum with a speed of 299792458 (i.e., approximately 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108)m/s.
  • Light always travels in a straight line. For example, formation of shadow shows that light travels in a straight line.

Characteristics of an Image formed by a plane mirror
The image formed by a plane mirror is

  1. Virtual and erect
  2. Of the same size as the object
  3. Laterally inverted i.e., left right interchanged
  4. As far behind the mirror, as the object is in front of the mirror

Formation of Images on a concave mirror when object between focus and center of curvature.

When the object is between C and F
Formation of Images on a concave mirror when Object at focus.
Formation of Images on a concave mirror when object place in between pole and focus.
Sign conventions for spherical mirrors:-
Convex Mirror: A convex mirror is that spherical mirror in which the process of reflection takes place at the convex or bulging out surface.
Concave Mirror: A concave mirror is that spherical mirror in which the process of reflection takes place at the concave or curved surface.
Spherical Mirror: Spherical mirror is that mirror whose reflecting surface is a portion of a hallow sphere of glass. It is of two main types, concave mirror and convex mirror.
Mirror: A mirror is a piece of glass having at least one polished surface and one reflecting surface.

TERMS RELATED TO SPHERICAL MIRROR

  • Pole(P) - Centre of the reflecting surface
  • Centre of curvature(C) - Centre of the hollow sphere of which spherical mirror is a part
  • Radius of curvature(R) - Radius of the sphere of which spherical mirror is a part (R=2f)
  • Principal axis - Straight line passing through the pole and center of curvature
  • Principal focus - Point at which light rays converge or appear to converge after reflection
  • Focal length - Distance between the pole and the principal focus
  • Aperture - The diameter of the reflecting surface is defined as aperture
Formation of Images on a concave mirror when object at center of curvature.
Formation of Images on a concave mirror when object beyond center of curvature.
Formation of Images on a concave mirror when object at infinity.
Images formed by a convex mirror

USE OF CONCAVE MIRROR

  1. Concave mirror is used in torches, search-lights and vehicles headlights to get powerful parallel beams of light.
  2. It is used as shaving mirrors and dentist mirror to see a larger image of the face.
  3. It is used in solar furnace to converge sunlight.

USE OF CONVEX MIRROR
Convex mirrors are commonly used as rear-view mirrors in vehicle as it enables the driver to view much larger area.

Mirror formula:-

If the image distance of a mirror be represented by u and the focal length by f, then mirror formula can be represented as under:-
{tex}\frac{1}{u} + \frac1v = \frac1f{/tex}
Where v = distance of the image from the mirror.
And u = object distance from the mirror.
Also f = focal length of the mirror.

Linear magnification of a mirror:- Linear magnification of a mirror is the ratio of height of the image to the total height of the object, thus,
Linear magnification = {tex}\frac{Height \; of \; Image}{Height \;of \; Object}{/tex}

Refraction: When a ray of light travelling in a homogenous medium, strikes a surface of separation between the two media, passes through it and goes into the second medium. The phenomenon is referred to as refraction.

Laws Of Refraction:

  1. The incident ray, the refracted ray and the normal to the interface of two transparent media at the point of incidence, all lie in the same plane.
  2. The ratio of sine of angle of incidence to the sine of angle of refraction is a constant, for the light of a given colour and for the given pair of media. This law is also known as Snell’s law of refraction. {tex}\frac{Sin \; i}{Sin \; r}{/tex} = Constant

Refractive Index : Refractive index of a medium is defined as the ratio of velocity of light in vacuum to the velocity of light in medium, i.e.,
{tex}\mathrm{u}=\frac{\text { Velocity of light in vacuume or air }}{\text { Velocity of light in given medium }}{/tex}
Lens:- A lens is a piece of transparent glass bounded by two spherical surfaces. It is of two main types viz.

Concave Lens: A lens having both of its surfaces curved inwards is called as a concave lens.
Kinds of concave lenses: these are of three kinds as explained under in the diagram:-
Convex Lens:- A lens having both of its surfaces bulged outwards is called as a convex lens.

TERMS RELATED TO SPHERICAL LENSES

  • Centre of Curvature(C) : Centre of two spheres, of which lens is a part Radius of Curvature(R) : The radii of spheres, of which lens is a part
  • Principal Axis: Line joining the centres of curvature of two surfaces of lens
  • Optical Centre (O) : Centre point of lens, light passes through it without deviation
  • Principal Focus: Point on the principal axis at which all incident rays converge or appear to diverge after refraction through the lens
  • Focal length (f) : Distance between the optical centre and the focus.
The position, size and the nature of the image formed by a convex lens for various positions of the object.

Nature, position and relative size of the image formed by a concave lens
Lens Formula and Magnification

Uses of Concave Lens

  1. It is used in spectacles for correcting the short-sightedness.
  2. It is used in laboratories.

Lens formula for spherical lenses
{tex}\frac1v - \frac1u=\frac1f{/tex}

Magnification
Magnification = {tex}\frac{height \; of \;image}{height \; of \; object}{/tex}
{tex} M = \frac{h'}{h}{/tex}
Magnification (m) = {tex}\frac{h’}{h} = \frac{v}{u}{/tex}

POWER OF LENS
The power of a lens is defined as the reciprocal of its focal length.
It is represented by the letter P.
 {tex}P = \frac1f{/tex} (focal length is in meter)
The SI unit of power of a lens is ‘diopter’. It is denoted by the letter D.
